cause: the spec gate's structural edit-class check was run against a stale remote ref and its verdict was reported as if earned. The branch sat 13 commits behind the target, and the ref the diff resolved against had never been refreshed, so the check compared the draft to a 77-scenario base when the real base carried 90. It answered addOnly true — the same answer the honest check later gave — which is exactly why it was dangerous: the claim was right by luck and unverified in substance, and the freeze self-clear rested on it. The cold judge caught it by reading the remote itself rather than trusting the number. Rebased onto the true tip and re-ran: 90 unchanged, 16 added, 0 modified, 0 removed. The base of a structural diff is a fact with its own freshness, not a constant, and a check whose base is stale reports the shape of the wrong question.

two new scenarios were inert against the wrong implementation they exist to catch, both the single-brancher shape. The verb outline and the single-match scenario each fixed a world of exactly one live pane, so a resolver that ignored the name argument entirely and acted on the sole live pane passed all nine rows; nothing set up two differently-labeled panes and asserted the named one was chosen over the others. Separately the by-existence-not-by-shape scenario asserted a mechanism rather than an observable, with a Given vague enough ('a string that resolves to a pane') that a syntax-sniffing resolver passed it too. Fixed by giving each a world that discriminates: three distinctly-labeled panes with the other two asserted untouched, and an id-shaped label that no pane carries as an id, which a sniffer reports as not-found and a live-list resolver resolves.

the amendment adding a third exit code to the shared output contract did not clear the bar its own cited precedent set. That contract's node records it as the same contract two sibling projects adopted, and the earlier amendment to it — the one this change invoked as precedent — hedges explicitly that nothing is asserted about the other adopters and files a follow-up. This one asserted its meaning held across every command with neither disclaimer, quietly widening a one-bin divergence into a claim on the contract itself. Scoped to this bin and the cross-adopter question filed, matching the precedent it had claimed to be following.

the reference node was written in the past tense about an implementation that does not exist. This is a spec-only round with no source change at all — the CLI's pane-target helper still wraps the raw string unchanged — yet the node claimed the structured error was 'realized today' and 'built', and said the structured-error clause was 'now realized on exactly one path'. The claim landed in the very section whose job is telling a future reader what is contract and what ships, so it would have frozen a false build report into the one place the impl gate reads as ground truth, and no test could have caught it because the artifact is prose. Rewritten to contract tense: specified, not yet built.

the false-built tense corrected in the reference node recurred in this CR's own durable records, which asserted in the present tense that the ambiguity report 'is the first structured error on this surface' and that the clause 'is realized on one path'. The same defect class the previous round existed to kill, surviving in a different file because the fix had been applied to the two instances named rather than swept for. Corrected to contract tense, and the sweep run across every touched artifact rather than the flagged lines.

three correction lines were written to the durable ledger shard, which holds only the run-level leash, self-asserted gate verdicts, and follow-up records. A correction belongs to the combat log beside the plan brief, and the distinction is not cosmetic: the ledger outlives the mission while the combat log is distilled and dropped at retro, so a correction filed there would have leaked mission-local iteration into the permanent record. Every other shard in the corpus gets this right; this one was the sole outlier. Moved here, with the seq and ts the combat-log shape requires and the ledger left holding only what it owns.

the impl gate cut the listing's label column for breaking the output contract's three-to-four field ceiling, and the requester kicked the cut back with the fact that resolves it: the column that should have gone was the multiplexer's. One adapter is selected per session, so every row of a listing reports the SAME mux — it is constant by construction and discriminates nothing, which is the identical argument this CR had already used to keep mux out of the ambiguity report's candidate fields and then failed to apply one surface over. Swapped rather than added: the row stays at four fields, the ceiling holds, and the label a caller now types is visible where they would look for it. The follow-up recorded at handoff is dropped rather than filed, being no longer outstanding. Also corrected while here: that example named pane, mux, harness and was stale on two counts — cwd had long been a fourth column, and mux was noise.

the delivery's new tests leaked a fixture's fake pane output into the test runner's own stdout, and both gates missed it. The read verb writes a pane's captured bytes straight to stdout rather than through the logger — the output contract's one deliberate raw-stream exception — while the CLI test harness spied only the logger and stderr. No test had ever driven read before this CR, so the hole was invisible until these did, and it surfaced as a bare word appearing in every verify run rather than as a failure. Caught only by reading the runner's output rather than its summary line. Nearly dismissed as pre-existing on a comparison against the target that reported zero occurrences — but that run's test COUNT was never checked, and checking it is what proved the comparison sound (100 tests on the target with no leak, 129 here with one) rather than a suite that had silently failed to run. The same unverified-claim shape this CR kept hitting, one layer down in the tooling that checks for it. The harness now spies stdout; a test asserting on stdout still spies it locally, and a mutation confirmed those assertions still bite rather than being silenced by the fix.

the whole exit-code rationale rested on a fact never checked at the source, and the requester caught it after the PR merged. AXI defines 0 = success, 1 = error, 2 = USAGE ERROR. This CR read the adopting node's incomplete restatement of that set (0/1 only), concluded a third code did not exist, invented one meaning couldn't-answer, and wrote a scoped amendment plus a cross-adopter divergence follow-up to justify it — an elaborate apparatus built on the absence of a paragraph nobody had read. The research that should have caught it fetched the framework's landing page rather than its specification, reported that no code taxonomy was detailed there, and that absence was taken as evidence the taxonomy did not exist. Then, asked point blank whether exit 2 collided, the answer given was that nothing conflicted and the risk had been overstated — a confident all-clear on the unread question. Four cold judge rounds and an impl judge all passed it, because every one of them graded against the adopting node rather than the framework it claims to adopt. The code turns out to be RIGHT and only its label wrong: an ambiguous locator is a usage error in the strict sense AXI means, so the suite needed no change. The real divergences were the ones hiding behind the invented one — errors on stderr where AXI says stdout and says agents do not read stderr, and usage errors at 1 where AXI says 2 — both spanning the whole verb surface, both older than this CR, and neither noticed while its author was busy defending a code that was never in dispute.
